---
title: "Loops and Tracing"
description: "Repeat work with for and while loops, control loop flow, and trace changing values."
execute:
  echo: true
categories: [core-python, beginner]
order: 120
---
::: {.lesson-meta}
**Level:** Beginner
**Estimated time:** 35–50 minutes
**You will learn:** Repeat work with for and while loops, control loop flow, and trace changing values.
**Practice in:** Google Colab, Jupyter, or VS Code
:::

## The problem this lesson solves

Computers are especially good at repetition. If you can write instructions for
one item, a loop can repeat those instructions for many items. Loops help you
print every name in a list, add many numbers, search for a match, or keep asking
until valid input appears.

The hard part for beginners is not typing `for` or `while`; it is tracking what
changes each time the loop repeats. This lesson teaches loops together with
**tracing**, the habit of writing down values as the program runs.

## Learning goals

By the end, you should be able to:

- write a `for` loop over a list;
- write a `while` loop with a clear stopping rule;
- explain the loop variable and the loop body;
- use `break` and `continue` intentionally;
- make a trace table to debug loop behavior.

## How loops repeat work

::: {.concept-diagram}
A loop is a repeated path. The important debugging question is: what changes each
time around the loop?

```{mermaid}
%%| echo: false
%%| eval: true
flowchart TD
  start([Start loop]) --> check{"Another item<br/>or condition true?"}
  check -- Yes --> bind["Set loop variable<br/>or inspect condition"]
  bind --> body["Run loop body"]
  body --> update["Update state<br/>total, count, index, etc."]
  update --> check
  check -- No --> done([Continue after loop])
```

Use a trace table beside this diagram: one row for each trip through the loop.
:::

## 1. `for` loops repeat once per item

A `for` loop is best when you already have a collection of items and want to do
something with each item.

```python
for item in collection:
    do_something_with(item)
```

Read this as: "for each item in this collection, run the indented block."

### First example

Predict the output before running the code.

```python
names = ["Ada", "Grace", "Katherine"]

for name in names:
    print(f"Hello, {name}")
```

### Step-by-step explanation

1. `names = ["Ada", "Grace", "Katherine"]`
   - Python creates a list of three strings.
2. `for name in names:`
   - Python starts the loop.
   - On the first trip, `name` refers to `"Ada"`.
3. `print(f"Hello, {name}")`
   - The indented line is the loop body.
   - It prints `Hello, Ada` on the first trip.
4. Python goes back to the top of the loop.
   - On the second trip, `name` refers to `"Grace"`.
   - On the third trip, `name` refers to `"Katherine"`.
5. After the last item, the loop ends.

### Trace table

| Trip through loop | `name` value | Printed line |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | `"Ada"` | `Hello, Ada` |
| 2 | `"Grace"` | `Hello, Grace` |
| 3 | `"Katherine"` | `Hello, Katherine` |

A trace table makes an invisible process visible.

### Building a total

Many loops update a value each time:

```python
scores = [10, 8, 9]
total = 0

for score in scores:
    total = total + score

print(total)
```

The variable `total` starts at `0`. Each trip through the loop replaces `total`
with a bigger value.

| Trip | `score` | old `total` | new `total` |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| start | — | — | `0` |
| 1 | `10` | `0` | `10` |
| 2 | `8` | `10` | `18` |
| 3 | `9` | `18` | `27` |

### Common mistakes

::: {.callout-warning title="Common mistake"}
**Mistake: forgetting to indent the loop body.**

```python
for name in names:
print(name)
```

The `print` line must be indented so Python knows it belongs inside the loop.
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="Common mistake"}
**Mistake: resetting the total inside the loop.**

```python
scores = [10, 8, 9]
for score in scores:
    total = 0
    total = total + score
print(total)
```

This prints only the last score because `total` becomes `0` on every trip. Put
`total = 0` before the loop.
:::

### Check your understanding

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "for-loops-checkpoint",
  "title": "For loops checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "loop-variable",
      "prompt": "In for name in names:, what happens to name?",
      "options": [
        "It refers to one item from names on each trip through the loop.",
        "It always refers to the first item only.",
        "It becomes the entire list permanently.",
        "It must be named item."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The loop variable is updated to the next item each time through the loop."
    },
    {
      "id": "total-start",
      "prompt": "Where should total = 0 go when adding all numbers in a list?",
      "options": [
        "Before the loop starts.",
        "Inside the loop as the first indented line.",
        "After the final print only.",
        "It is never needed."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The total should be initialized once before the loop, not reset every trip."
    },
    {
      "id": "trace-purpose",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of a trace table?",
      "options": [
        "To record changing values step by step.",
        "To make code run without errors automatically.",
        "To replace all tests.",
        "To hide output from the user."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Tracing makes the repeated steps visible."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## 2. `while` loops repeat while a condition is true

A `while` loop is best when you do not know ahead of time how many repetitions
you need. It keeps running while a condition remains true.

```python
while condition:
    body
```

A safe `while` loop usually has three parts:

1. a starting value;
2. a condition that can eventually become false;
3. an update inside the loop.

### First example

```python
count = 3

while count > 0:
    print(count)
    count = count - 1

print("Go!")
```

### Step-by-step explanation

1. `count = 3`
   - The loop starts with `count` equal to `3`.
2. `while count > 0:`
   - Python asks: "Is count greater than 0?"
   - If yes, the indented block runs.
3. `print(count)`
   - The current value is displayed.
4. `count = count - 1`
   - This update is the stopping progress. It moves `count` closer to `0`.
5. When `count` becomes `0`, `count > 0` is false and the loop stops.
6. Python continues after the loop and prints `Go!`.

### Trace table

| Check | `count > 0` | Printed | New `count` |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | `True` | `3` | `2` |
| 2 | `True` | `2` | `1` |
| 3 | `True` | `1` | `0` |
| 4 | `False` | loop stops | — |

### Infinite loops

An **infinite loop** happens when the condition never becomes false.

```python
count = 3
while count > 0:
    print(count)
```

This never changes `count`, so `count > 0` stays true forever. In notebooks,
you may need to interrupt or stop the cell.

### Check your understanding

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "while-loops-checkpoint",
  "title": "While loops checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "while-condition",
      "prompt": "When does a while loop stop?",
      "options": [
        "When its condition becomes false.",
        "After exactly one trip.",
        "Only when print() is called.",
        "When the variable name changes spelling."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "A while loop repeats while the condition is true and stops when it is false."
    },
    {
      "id": "infinite-loop",
      "prompt": "What is a common cause of an infinite while loop?",
      "options": [
        "The loop condition never becomes false.",
        "The loop has one print statement.",
        "The starting value is an integer.",
        "The code is inside a notebook."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Without an update that moves toward stopping, the condition may stay true forever."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## 3. `break` and `continue`

Sometimes a loop needs extra control:

- `break` exits the loop immediately;
- `continue` skips the rest of the current trip and starts the next trip.

Use them sparingly. If you use them, explain why.

### First example: stop when found

```python
names = ["Ada", "Grace", "Katherine"]

for name in names:
    if name == "Grace":
        print("Found Grace!")
        break
```

Python checks names in order. When `name` is `"Grace"`, it prints the message
and `break` stops the loop. `"Katherine"` is never checked.

### First example: skip one item

```python
scores = [10, -1, 8]

for score in scores:
    if score < 0:
        continue
    print(score)
```

The negative score is skipped. The loop still continues with later items.

### Common mistakes

::: {.callout-warning title="Common mistake"}
**Mistake: using `break` when you meant `continue`.**

If one bad item should be skipped, use `continue`. If the whole search should
stop, use `break`.
:::

### Check your understanding

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "loop-control-checkpoint",
  "title": "Loop control checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "break-meaning",
      "prompt": "What does break do inside a loop?",
      "options": [
        "It exits the loop immediately.",
        "It skips only the current item and continues.",
        "It converts strings to integers.",
        "It repeats the previous line."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "break stops the loop completely."
    },
    {
      "id": "continue-meaning",
      "prompt": "What does continue do inside a loop?",
      "options": [
        "It skips the rest of the current trip and moves to the next trip.",
        "It stops the entire program.",
        "It creates a list.",
        "It changes a for loop into a while loop."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "continue keeps the loop going but skips the remaining body for the current item."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## 4. Tracing loops when they surprise you

When a loop produces an unexpected result, do not guess randomly. Trace it.

### Debugging routine

1. Write down the variables that change.
2. Make a table with one row per loop trip.
3. Fill in values before and after the loop body.
4. Check whether the stopping condition eventually becomes false.
5. Add temporary `print()` calls if the table is hard to fill.

### Practice example

```python
numbers = [2, 4, 6]
total = 0

for number in numbers:
    total = total + number
    print("number:", number, "total:", total)

print("final:", total)
```

Temporary prints are like a flashlight. They help you see what the loop is doing.
After you understand the program, you can remove them.

### Check your understanding

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "loop-tracing-checkpoint",
  "title": "Tracing checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "changing-values",
      "prompt": "When tracing a loop, what should you write down first?",
      "options": [
        "The variables that change during the loop.",
        "Every Python keyword alphabetically.",
        "Only the final answer.",
        "The colors in your editor."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Tracing is useful because it tracks how values change step by step."
    },
    {
      "id": "temporary-print",
      "prompt": "Why might you add temporary print() calls while debugging a loop?",
      "options": [
        "To see values during each trip through the loop.",
        "To make the loop faster.",
        "To permanently change the algorithm.",
        "To avoid reading the code."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Temporary prints reveal what Python is doing while the loop runs."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Key points

::: {.callout-tip title="Key points"}
- A `for` loop repeats once for each item in a collection.
- A `while` loop repeats while a condition is true.
- Indentation defines the loop body.
- Values that change inside a loop should be traced carefully.
- `break` exits a loop; `continue` skips to the next trip.
- Trace tables are one of the best beginner debugging tools.
:::

## References

- Python Tutorial: `for`, `range`, `break`, and `continue`: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/controlflow.html
- Python Tutorial: data structures: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/datastructures.html
